content:
15 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Two weeks ago I published the story of building a complete LLM pipeline from scratch in Rust — pretraining, GRPO, Flash Attention v2, self-learning — with not a single line of Python anywhere in the stack. The most common question in the comments wasn’t “does it work?” It was “okay, but is it just a toy, or can this actually grow?”
aarambh-ai v2.0.0 is my answer. Thirteen pull requests, thirteen phases, and one production release later, the same architecture that once struggled to spell “Shakespeare” now sees images, calls functions with structurally-valid JSON, trains Mixture-of-Experts layers, splits work across two GPUs, and serves its own OpenAI-compatible HTTP API. Same 8GB i3 laptop for the parts that fit. Same free Kaggle sessions for the parts that don’t. Same rule: still zero Python, still zero pretrained weights shipped.
This post is the deep-dive on what changed, why each phase was sequenced the way it was, and the specific engineering problems that made each feature hard.

What Is aarambh-ai, in One Paragraph?
aarambh-ai (Sanskrit for beginning) is a decoder-only transformer LLM application built as a Rust workspace on top of the Candle tensor backend — no PyTorch, no ONNX Runtime, no Python FFI anywhere in the dependency graph. v1.0.0 covered the full text-only vertical: pretraining, SFT, LoRA/QLoRA, GRPO alignment, INT4/GGUF quantization, Flash Attention v2 CUDA kernels, a thinking engine, safety guardrails, and CPU-capable self-learning. v2.0.0 answers the natural next question: does this scale past “one modality, one GPU, one training recipe”?
Short answer: yes. Here’s how.

The Three Directions v2.0 Grew In
v2.0 isn’t one feature — it’s thirteen phases (16 through 28) organized around three axes:

• Deeper on text — longer context via RoPE scaling, a real evaluation harness instead of perplexity-only, a stronger fine-tuning method (DoRA), a second alignment path (DPO) alongside GRPO, and faster inference (speculative decoding, grammar-constrained tool calling).

• Wider — a new modality — a frozen vision encoder plus a trainable projector gives the model the ability to see and reason about images, trained entirely on free public datasets.

• Bigger training infrastructure — Mixture of Experts and multi-GPU data parallelism, both deliberately framed as systems-engineering exercises whose payoff is measured, not assumed.
That third point matters more than it sounds. Let me explain why.

The Discipline That Shaped Everything: Measure, Don’t Assume
v1.0.0 judged model quality with one number: perplexity on a holdout set. That’s a blunt signal — it tells you the model is getting better at predicting the next token, but not whether it can actually answer a multiple-choice question, solve a word problem, or write code that runs.
Phase 17 fixed that with aarambh-ai-eval — a proper evaluation harness with four free, public benchmark subsets sitting alongside PPL:
| Task | What it measures | Scoring |
|---|---|---|
| MMLU-lite | General knowledge, multiple choice | Next-token logprob comparison over A/B/C/D |
| HellaSwag | Commonsense sentence completion | Logprob ranking over candidate completions |
| GSM8K-subset | Grade-school math word problems | Exact-match, reusing the existing `MathVerifier` |
| HumanEval-lite | Python code generation | pass@1 via sandboxed execution |Every task implements one shared EvalTask trait, so new tasks — like Phase 20's vqa_subset for visual question answering — plug into the same harness runner without touching it:
trait EvalTask {
fn name(&self) -> &'static str;
fn run(&self, context: &EvalContext, config: &EvalConfig) -> TaskScore;
}Here’s why this comes before Mixture of Experts and multi-GPU in the roadmap, not after: every phase from 16 onward that changes model behavior has to report a before/after scorecard. aarambh-ai eval --compare before.json after.json is how Phase 22 (MoE) and Phase 24 (DPO) are judged — not by whether the feature sounds like it should help, but by whether the numbers actually move. At Tiny/Small scale, that's an honest question, and the harness is what makes it answerable instead of assumed.

Longer Context Without Retraining From Zero
v1’s RoPE computed fixed rotation frequencies once at max_seq_len. It works well inside the trained context window and degrades sharply past it — the model has literally never seen rotation angles for positions beyond what it trained on.
Phase 16 adds two RoPE scaling methods:
YaRN interpolates between the original and a scaled frequency, using a ramp function that leaves high-frequency (short-range) dimensions mostly untouched while stretching low-frequency (long-range) dimensions more aggressively:
For each RoPE dimension pair i:
if i is high frequency (< beta_fast): keep original θᵢ
if i is low frequency (> beta_slow): use θᵢ / scale_factor
in between: ramp linearly between the twoNTK-aware scaling is the simpler one-line alternative — just rescale rope_theta itself:
θ_new = rope_theta × factor^(d / (d − 2))NTK-aware is a config change you can try in five minutes. YaRN needs a short continued-pretraining pass on long documents but generalizes better. Both are implemented behind RopeScalingConfig.method, and method: None reproduces v1.0.0's RoPE output exactly — enforced by a dedicated regression test, because "we added a feature and silently changed old behavior" is exactly the kind of bug that hides for months.
The practical upshot: context grows through a progressive 4K → 8K → 16K schedule during continued pretraining, KV caches are now preallocated at max_seq_len instead of growing by concatenation on every token, and the model never materializes a full [max_seq_len × max_seq_len] causal mask anymore — attention dispatch just passes causal = true straight to the kernel.

DoRA: A Stronger LoRA, Not a Replacement
v1’s LoRA adapts a frozen weight W₀ with a low-rank update: W = W₀ + BA. The problem is that this update changes a weight vector's magnitude and direction together, coupled through the same low-rank matrices.
DoRA (Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation) decomposes the weight first:
W₀ = m · (V / ||V||_c) where m = magnitude, V/||V||_c = unit directionDoRA update:
direction' = (V + BA) / ||V + BA||_c ← low-rank update, but only to direction
m' = trainable magnitude vector ← full-rank, trained independently
W' = m' · direction'
Magnitude and direction can now move independently instead of being locked together. Published results show this consistently beats plain LoRA at the same rank, for the cost of one extra magnitude vector per adapted layer — a negligible memory addition on top of LoRA’s footprint. finetune dora and finetune qdora sit right alongside finetune sft, finetune lora, and finetune qlora as genuine alternatives, not replacements. The eval harness decides which one wins for a given run — that's not a rhetorical flourish, it's Phase 18's actual acceptance criterion.

Teaching a Text-Only Model to See
This is the phase I was most nervous about, because “add vision to an LLM” is where a lot of from-scratch projects either quietly reach for PyTorch bindings or quietly give up.
The constraint I set for myself: no tch-rs, no ONNX Runtime, no Python FFI, anywhere. So Phase 19 follows the LLaVA recipe instead of trying to train a vision encoder from scratch — which would need dataset scale and compute far beyond what free Kaggle sessions offer:
Image (H×W×3)
│
▼
Patchify + linear patch embed (frozen) → N patches × vit_d_model
│
▼
ViT transformer blocks (frozen, pretrained CLIP-B/32 weights, ~86M params)
│
▼
Projector MLP (trainable): vit_d_model → hidden → llm_d_model
│
▼
N "image tokens" in llm_d_model space
│
▼
Spliced into the text sequence at the <image> special token position
│
▼
...the rest of aarambh-ai's decoder, completely unmodified from v1...A frozen, pretrained CLIP-B/32-scale encoder turns an image into a grid of patch embeddings. A small trainable projector MLP maps those into the language model’s d_model space, so each image patch looks — to the rest of the decoder — like an ordinary token embedding. The CLIP weights load as SafeTensors directly through candle-core, the exact same loading path aarambh-ai-weights already used for the language model in v1. The only new dependency is the image crate, used purely for local decode/resize/normalize. No network calls, no external service, no policy violated.
I chose prefix fusion (image tokens spliced directly into the input sequence) over a Flamingo-style cross-attention mechanism. It’s the deliberately simpler option: zero changes to TransformerBlock, GroupedQueryAttention, or the KV cache — as far as the decoder is concerned, image tokens are just tokens. The trade-off is that images now consume context budget alongside text, which is part of why long context (Phase 16) is sequenced before vision — more room for image and text to coexist.
Training happens in two stages, mirroring LLaVA’s own recipe: first the projector trains alone with everything else frozen (Phase 19, captioning only), then the projector trains alongside a DoRA-adapted LLM for open-ended visual question answering (Phase 20). Using DoRA rather than full fine-tuning here isn’t a stylistic choice — full fine-tuning of even the Small scale on image-text pairs would blow straight through free Kaggle quota.
The thinking engine needed zero changes for any of this. Because fusion happens before the decoder even sees the sequence, a <think> block generated after image tokens behaves identically to one generated after text-only context. The ThinkingController doesn't know or care that some of the preceding context came from a picture.

Mixture of Experts: An Honest Framing
Here’s where I made a deliberate choice to be more careful with claims than most MoE writeups are. MoE’s whole value proposition is routing to specialized experts across a large total parameter count while keeping active compute per token low. At Tiny/Small scale (25M–117M dense-equivalent), there isn’t a lot of room for experts to meaningfully specialize.
So Phase 22 is documented, explicitly, as a systems-engineering exercise first and a quality improvement second — with the eval harness deciding which framing turns out to be true for a given run, rather than me asserting it in the release notes.
MoeConfig { num_experts, top_k, expert_ffn_dim, aux_loss_weight, every_n_layers }Router: linear gate, d_model → num_experts logits per token
top_k_gating(logits) → indices + softmax weights, over the top_k experts only
MoeFfn: num_experts independent SwiGLU FFNs, each token dispatched to its
top_k experts and weighted-summed
Load-balancing auxiliary loss: penalizes uneven expert utilization,
added to the main cross-entropy loss
MoeFfn is a drop-in replacement for the dense SwiGluFfn at the block level — same input/output shape, selected every N layers via moe: Option<MoeConfig> on the model config. moe: None reproduces v1's dense FFN exactly. v2.0 ships a dense masked-matmul dispatch — every expert computes on every token, then gets masked and weighted by the router — as the first, simplest-correct implementation. Sparse/grouped dispatch, where only the selected experts actually compute, is called out explicitly as a future optimization, not a Phase 22 requirement. Correctness first, efficiency second.

Two GPUs, One Model: Multi-GPU Data Parallelism
Kaggle occasionally grants 2×T4 sessions instead of the usual single GPU, and I didn’t want that extra hardware sitting idle. Phase 23 adds single-node NCCL data-parallel training: deterministic sharded data loaders so each rank sees a disjoint slice of the batch, gradient averaging across ranks, rank-zero logging and checkpointing so you don’t get duplicate log spam or checkpoint races, and a clean single-GPU fallback when the second card isn’t available. Multi-node training is explicitly out of scope — this is single-node, multi-GPU only.

DPO: A
